title: A Bright Star's Hot Mystery Solved
summary: A star named Gamma Cassiopeiae, which you can see in the sky, has been puzzling scientists for 50 years because it gives off very strong and hot X-rays that are unusual for a star like it. Astronomers used a special telescope called XRISM to look at the star and found out that the X-rays come from a small, dense star called a white dwarf that orbits around it. This white dwarf has a magnetic field that pulls material from the bigger star, creating the hot X-rays. The discovery helps scientists understand a new type of star system and opens up more questions about how stars change over time. The research was done by a team from the University of Liège and published in a science journal.

keywords.explanation: a big ball of gas in space that shines, like our Sun
a type of energy that is very hot and strong, used by doctors to see inside bodies
a small, dense star that is very old and no longer shines brightly
to move around another object in space, like the Earth goes around the Sun
an invisible force that can pull things, like a magnet
scientists who study stars and space

questions.question: What is the name of the star that scientists studied?
What unusual thing does Gamma Cassiopeiae do?
What did astronomers find orbiting the star?
What instrument helped solve the mystery?
What does the white dwarf's magnetic field do?

background_read: Stars are giant balls of gas in space that give off light and heat. Some stars, like Gamma Cassiopeiae, are very big and spin fast, throwing material into space. X-rays are a type of energy that is very hot and can come from stars, but Gamma Cassiopeiae makes much stronger X-rays than expected. Astronomers use telescopes to study stars and solve mysteries about them. This discovery helps us learn about how stars work together in pairs called binary systems.
Article_Structure: WHO: Astronomers from the University of Liège
WHAT: Solved why Gamma Cassiopeiae star has extreme X-rays by finding a white dwarf orbiting it
WHERE: In the constellation Cassiopeia, observed from space with XRISM telescope
WHY: To understand a 50-year mystery and learn about new types of star systems

perspectives.description: Scientists are excited because they solved a long-standing puzzle about a star, showing how teamwork and new technology can reveal secrets of the universe.
